decision_simulation: The real historical moment: June 1948, occupied West Germany. Allied administrators must decide whether to proceed with an entirely new currency reform in the western occupation zones — a move that would formally split Germany's currency (and likely deepen the East-West divide) right as Soviet forces watched closely. The outcome was genuinely uncertain: the Soviet Union responded within days by blockading West Berlin, testing whether the Western Allies would abandon the city rather than risk war.Player choice: Proceed with the West German currency reform despite the risk of Soviet retaliation, or delay reform to avoid provoking a crisis.Historical resolution: The Allies proceeded with the reform on June 20, 1948. The Soviet Union responded with the Berlin Blockade just days later, and the Allies answered with the Berlin Airlift rather than abandoning the city.

understand.economic_impact: The June 1948 currency reform is widely credited as the foundation of West Germany's "Wirtschaftswunder" (economic miracle), transforming empty, hoarded shelves into stocked stores almost overnight once money regained real value Bundesbank official history. The Deutsche Mark went on to become one of Europe's most respected and stable currencies for the remainder of the 20th century [TH Script Writing Bible voice profile].
understand.political_impact: The currency reform directly triggered the Soviet Union's Berlin Blockade (1948-49), one of the first major crises of the Cold War, met by the Western Allies with the Berlin Airlift rather than withdrawal Truman Library. The reform effectively formalized the economic (and soon political) division between West and East Germany.
understand.why_it_still_matters: Imagine a country so broken that store shelves are empty, not because there's nothing to sell, but because nobody trusts the money enough to sell it for. That was Germany in 1948, still living with the memory of 1923, when a loaf of bread cost 200 billion of the old currency. When a brand new currency was introduced almost overnight, with a small, guaranteed amount of money given to everyone, something remarkable happened — people started trusting money again, and stores filled back up almost immediately. Klaus's story matters because it shows that money is fundamentally about trust — when people believe a currency will hold its value, an entire economy can start working again almost overnight; when they don't, even full shelves might as well be empty.
